text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Amelia Turner  
St. Bartholomew’s Hospital  
Department of Neurology, West Smithfield, London EC1A 7BE  

Dear Dr Amelia Turner,  

Margaret Collins, DOB: 14-03-1956, Hospital No: H392745 NHS No. 9001845672  
22 Brookfield Close, Hampstead, London NW3 4JP 

Diagnoses:  

Primary generalised epilepsy with generalised spike-and-wave discharges on prior EEG (most recent EEG within normal limits between events).  

Current Medication:  

• Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily  
• Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily ï¿½  

Plan:  

• Continue current anti-seizure medications without change.  
• Reinforce regular sleep, hydration, and stress-reduction strategies; maintain seizure diary.  
• Safety counselling reiterated (driving regulations, bathing supervision where appropriate, and avoidance of high-risk activities during vulnerable periods).  
• Bloods (U&E, LFTs) in 6 months to monitor therapy tolerance.  
• Routine follow-up in 9–12 months, sooner if breakthrough clusters increase.  

I reviewed Margaret Collins in clinic today. Since retiring earlier this year, she reports a markedly more regular daily routine with consistent sleep and reduced occupational stress Ã‚Â·. She feels her seizure control has improved overall and notes better energy, fewer prodromal symptoms, and enhanced confidence in day-to-day activities. Notably, her seizure pattern has remained stereotyped: She may remain seizure-free for up to four month, but then will experience clusters of several seizures in a single day. These clusters have become less disruptive, typically occurring later in the day, and she has identified missed meals and acute stress as possible precipitants. There have been no injuries, no tongue biting of concern, and recovery times are shorter than previously reported. 

Her past history, examination today, and previous documentation remain in keeping with generalised epilepsy. She has had no adverse effects from Lamotrigine or Levetiracetam—no rash, mood change, or cognitive slowing—Ã© and her medication adherence is good. We discussed lifestyle measures, including maintaining her now-regular sleep–wake pattern post-retirement, structured mealtimes, and gentle aerobic exercise, all of which she finds helpful. She will continue to keep a seizure diary, particularly around the times when clusters have historically occurred, and she will alert us if the frequency or severity changes. 

In view of the clinical improvement associated with retirement and her more stable routine, I have not recommended any medication adjustments today. We will review blood tests in primary care at six months and I will see her again in 9–12 months. She is aware of DVLA guidance and confirms she is not currently driving. Should clusters become more frequent or prolonged, she will contact the service promptly for earlier review â€ . 

With best wishes,  

Yours sincerely

attributes.Label: 1 cluster per 4 month, multiple per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly describes the current pattern: the patient may remain seizure-free for up to four months, followed by clusters of several seizures in a single day. This indicates a cluster pattern with an interval of approximately four months between clusters. The exact number of seizures per cluster is not specified beyond 'several', which is vague. Therefore, the normalized label should be in the cluster format with frequency of 1 cluster per 4 months and 'multiple' per cluster, since the precise count is not given. No other competing frequencies are provided. She is not seizure-free for 6 months, so not considered seizure-free.
seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per 4 month, multiple per cluster
